summary: A small two-story rectangular building with brick-patterned lower and upper walls, horizontal banding (light-colored trim and wooden plank bands), corner stone pillars, a flat dark roof inset with a stone slab border, and a low surrounding white border at ground level. Windows run in a continuous row on both floors; there is no visible main door on the shown facades.

shape.overall_form: simple rectangular box with two stacked floors and a recessed flat roof
shape.symmetry: largely symmetric on the shown long facades (mirrored left-right), corners reinforced

materials.name: quartz
materials.role: horizontal trim / ground border / top coping (light smooth blocks)

materials.name: wood
materials.role: mid-level horizontal band (planks) and lower sill

elements: continuous horizontal rows of small rectangular windows on both floors
horizontal light-colored trim bands above and below the central wooden band
wooden plank band between floors acting as sill/trim
flat dark central roof area surrounded by stone slab coping
white low border wall around building at ground level
rebuild_hints: Build a rectangular foundation with a one-block-high white/quartz border inset from the outer trench as shown.
Walls: two stacked brick-clad courses separated by a full-width wooden plank band; place light smooth trim (quartz) strips above each brick course.
Place stonebrick corner pillars rising full building height interrupting the brick courses at each corner.
Create continuous 1x1 windows (glass) spaced evenly along each long facade within the brick courses.
Top: place a rim of stone slabs around the roof edge and fill the center with dark nether-brick (or similar dark block) flat surface.
uncertainties: Exact block types: light trim appears to be quartz (or smooth stone); certainty ~0.9 but could be another light smooth block.
Roof material: looks like nether brick for the dark field and stone slabs for the rim; could be another dark block and slab material ~0.8 confidence.
Exact footprint dimensions (width/depth) and internal layout are estimated from visible facades and top-down inset; actual interior divisions and any rear facade details are not visible.
